front: Work: In this 2010 MoMA performance, the artist sat in silence across from individual museum visitors for a combined 736 hours.
Work: During this performance, the former collaborator Ulay made a surprise appearance, leading to a tearful reunion that onlookers applauded.
Work: Lady Gaga helped popularize interest in this artist's 2010 MoMA retrospective performance.
Artist: a Tumblr blog called '[this artist] Made Me Cry' features photos by Marco Anelli captioned with the time it took visitors to cry at her exhibition
Artist: several commentators compared Shia LaBeouf's performance #IAMSORRY to this artist's work
Work: In this 1974 performance, the artist placed 72 objects on a table — including a loaded pistol with one bullet — and invited the audience to use them on her for six hours.
Work: During this performance, an audience member held a loaded gun to the artist's head, provoking a fight among other participants.
Work: During this performance, someone cut the artist's neck and drank her blood.
Work: During this performance, the artist's clothes were cut away with razor blades and a loaded gun was eventually forced into her hand.
Work: included bread, wine, and honey among the objects available to participants in this 1974 performance
Work: In 2005, this series of performances at the Guggenheim recreated notable works by other artists, including Nauman, Acconci, Beuys, Pane, and Export.
Work: The artist was denied permission to reproduce Chris Burden's Trans-Fixed for this 2005 Guggenheim series.
Work: This series of recreated performances was dedicated to Susan Sontag and included How to Explain Pictures to a Dead Hare.
Work: Inspired by a poster for the original, the artist held a gun while recreating Valie Export's Action Pants: Genital Panic as part of this series.
Work: On the last two nights of this Guggenheim series, the artist performed Thomas Lips and Entering the Other Side.
Work: This series of etchings features recipes like 'golden ball' supposedly sourced from Tibetan monks and later evolved into dinner party entertainment.
Work: A leaked email inviting John Podesta to this event led Alex Jones to link the artist and Hillary Clinton to the occult.
Work: A book associated with this work instructs the reader to obtain '13,000 grams of jealousy' to make 'aphrodisiac recipes.'
Artist: with pig blood, this artist wrote directions ostensibly for use in a kitchen for a work dubbed 'Spirit'
Work: In this collaborative piece, the artist leaned backwards holding a bow while her partner held an arrow pointed at her chest for four minutes.
Work: In this piece, the artist and her partner walked across the Great Wall of China from opposite ends and met in the middle to end their relationship.
Work: This artist and her partner formed a collective being known as 'the Other' throughout the 1970s.
Work: In this collaborative piece, the two artists stood naked on either side of a doorway while visitors squeezed past them.
Artist: Ulay sued this artist over breach of contract in 2015
Work: In this piece, the artist spent nineteen minutes breathing into her partner's mouth.
back: Breathing In / Breathing Out (Marina Abramović and Ulay)

front: Work: In this debut piece performed in Edinburgh, the artist played the knife game (five-finger fillet) with 20 different knives.
Work: While performing this piece, the artist lost consciousness after setting fire to a wooden star and lying inside it.
Work: This performance piece involved scrubbing thousands of bloody cow bones over four days, inspired by a war; the artist interviewed a rat-catcher for it.
Work: This series of 5-day workshops required participants to refrain from eating or speaking.
Artist: created a series of alabaster self-portraits titled Five Stages of Maya Dance
Artist: Denmark's Louisiana Museum of Art hosted this artist's first European retrospective in 2017, titled The Cleaner
Artist: This Serbian artist is widely called the 'grandmother of performance art.'
Artist: In a TED talk, this artist distinguished performance art from theater by saying 'the blood is the material,' not ketchup.
Artist: This performance artist called Taiwanese-born artist Tehching Hsieh a 'master.'
